name: Recruitment Channel Diversification
consequences: Pulling this lever expands the participant pool beyond current referral sources, potentially increasing enrollment speed but may dilute screening quality and increase administrative burden.
options: Leverage regional sleep disorder support groups to identify potential participants, offering informational sessions and screening questionnaires to broaden reach.
Partner with primary care practices to embed brief parasomnia screening tools in routine visits, funneling referrals directly to the study.
Implement a targeted online outreach campaign using patient forums and social media, directing interested adults to a dedicated study portal for pre-screening.
review: Tension between enrollment speed and screening rigor; options overlook the risk of enrolling participants with incomplete baseline data, potentially compromising data integrity.

name: Staffing Model Optimization
consequences: Adjusting staffing shifts can improve coverage and reduce overtime costs but may affect technician fatigue and data annotation consistency.
options: Introduce a rotating 12-hour shift schedule for technicians, ensuring continuous coverage while allowing for adequate rest periods.
Hire a dedicated night supervisor to oversee all suites, centralizing decision-making and reducing response time to flagged events.
Cross-train research assistants in basic PSG setup, enabling flexible staffing during peak enrollment periods without compromising quality.
review: Balancing coverage with technician well-being; options miss the potential for increased training costs and variable skill levels across staff.

name: Data Acquisition Protocol Flexibility
consequences: Allowing participants to opt for fewer enhanced nights reduces equipment wear and participant burden but may limit high-resolution event capture, affecting phenotyping depth.
options: Offer participants the choice to skip the mid-stay PSG night if they report low event frequency, reallocating resources to new enrollments.
Implement a dynamic scheduling algorithm that assigns enhanced nights based on real-time event detection, optimizing data yield per participant.
Provide a hybrid monitoring mode where participants wear a simplified PSG montage only during high-risk periods identified by actigraphy.
review: Trade-off between data richness and participant comfort; options fail to address the risk of missing rare events due to reduced PSG nights.

name: Event‑Triage Algorithm Development Path
consequences: Accelerating algorithm development can reduce manual scoring workload but may introduce false positives if early models are insufficiently validated.
options: Deploy a rule-based scoring system using thresholded sensor metrics to flag potential events for human review, reducing annotation load.
Collaborate with a computational neuroscience partner to develop a machine-learning model trained on annotated data, iterating with cross-validation.
Establish a crowdsourced annotation platform where trained volunteers assist in preliminary event labeling, accelerating dataset labeling.
review: Speeding algorithm deployment versus accuracy; options overlook the need for rigorous validation against gold-standard scoring before clinical use.

name: Participant Compensation Structure
consequences: Adjusting compensation rates can influence enrollment motivation but may affect budget sustainability and perceived study fairness.
options: Increase nightly compensation to €100 while reducing meal provision, balancing participant incentive with cost containment.
Introduce a tiered bonus system rewarding participants who complete full 8-week stays, encouraging retention without inflating baseline rates.
Offer non-monetary incentives such as sleep health reports and personalized feedback, enhancing participant value perception without additional cash outlay.
review: Balancing motivation and budget; options miss potential regulatory scrutiny over differential compensation impacting study equity.

name: Facility Safety and Environmental Design
consequences: Enhancing safety features can reduce incident risk but may increase renovation costs and alter the domestic feel, potentially affecting naturalistic behavior.
options: Install motion-activated lighting throughout suites to improve safety during nighttime awakenings for participants without compromising quietness.
Add discreet safety rails along corridor edges, ensuring participant mobility while preserving the residential aesthetic.
Implement a real-time environmental monitoring system that alerts staff to temperature or humidity deviations, safeguarding equipment and participant comfort.
review: Safety versus naturalistic environment; options overlook the potential for increased noise or visual cues that could alter sleep behavior.

name: Sleep Suite Utilization Optimization
consequences: Increasing suite utilization raises participant throughput and data yield, but simultaneous monitoring can introduce electromagnetic interference and acoustic overlap, potentially degrading event detection accuracy. Higher occupancy may also strain staff coverage and increase overtime costs, impacting the budget.
options: Stagger occupancy schedules across suites, allowing only two participants per night to reduce simultaneous monitoring load and preserve data fidelity.
Deploy portable isolation pods within each suite, enabling simultaneous full PSG recordings without cross‑suite electromagnetic interference.
Introduce a rotating night‑shift technician pool that cross‑trains on multiple suites, maintaining coverage while reducing overtime costs.
review: Balancing throughput against data integrity is the core tension. Options miss the effect of suite acoustic isolation on participant sleep quality.

name: Participant Retention Incentive Structure
consequences: Enhanced incentives can lower dropout rates and extend stay durations, increasing event capture, but they raise participant compensation costs and may attract individuals motivated primarily by rewards, potentially biasing the sample. Higher incentives also risk regulatory scrutiny regarding undue influence.
options: Offer tiered meal vouchers tied to stay duration, encouraging participants to remain longer and increasing data yield.
Provide personalized sleep hygiene workshops during stay, enhancing perceived value and reducing dropout rates.
Implement a gamified sleep diary reward system using non‑digital tokens to motivate nightly compliance and data completeness.
review: Incentivizing retention versus maintaining sample representativeness is the core tension. Options ignore potential bias introduced by differential incentive uptake.

name: Data Annotation Workflow Automation
consequences: Automating parts of the annotation workflow speeds up review and reduces human labor, but may introduce systematic errors if the pre‑scoring rules are not well calibrated, potentially lowering inter‑rater reliability. Initial development and validation of the automation engine also consume significant resources.
options: Deploy a rule‑based pre‑scoring engine that flags high‑probability events for rapid human review.
Introduce a peer‑review double‑scoring system where technicians cross‑check each other’s annotations in real time.
Adopt a continuous learning feedback loop where annotation errors are fed back into the scoring algorithm to improve future accuracy.
review: Speeding annotation versus preserving inter‑rater reliability is the core tension. Options overlook the need for ongoing calibration sessions.

name: Ethics Oversight Cadence
consequences: Increasing the frequency and depth of ethics oversight can improve compliance and participant safety, but it adds administrative load and may delay protocol amendments, slowing recruitment and study progress. More frequent audits also require additional staff time and may strain the project’s governance structure.
options: Establish a quarterly ethics audit that consolidates all protocol amendments into a single submission.
Create a real‑time ethics monitoring dashboard that flags potential violations before they occur.
Engage a dedicated ethics liaison who coordinates with the university IRB to streamline communication and reduce turnaround time.
review: Enhancing oversight versus administrative efficiency is the core tension. Options miss the impact of increased audit frequency on staff morale.

name: Infrastructure Resilience Planning
consequences: Building redundant power, network, and backup systems protects data integrity during outages but increases upfront capital expenditures and ongoing maintenance costs. Over‑engineering may divert funds from participant recruitment or data analysis activities.
options: Install redundant power feeds with battery backup for each suite to prevent data loss during outages.
Implement a modular network architecture that allows rapid replacement of failed components without full system downtime.
Conduct quarterly disaster‑recovery drills that test data restoration from encrypted backups to validate resilience protocols.
review: Building resilience versus budget constraints is the core tension. Options ignore the long‑term maintenance costs of redundant systems.

name: Control Group Matching Precision
consequences: Precise matching of controls to participants enhances internal validity but can reduce recruitment speed and increase exclusion rates, potentially limiting sample size. The additional screening burden may also raise participant burden and affect willingness to enroll.
options: Use a propensity‑score matching algorithm to select controls with similar baseline sleep metrics from the sleep clinic database.
Recruit matched controls through a dedicated volunteer registry that tracks sleep patterns and demographics in real time.
Offer a brief overnight screening PSG to all potential controls to confirm absence of parasomnia before enrollment.
review: Matching precision versus recruitment velocity is the core tension. Options overlook the potential for selection bias introduced by screening PSG.

name: Participant Screening Efficiency
consequences: Pulling this lever will streamline pre-admission screening, reducing time from referral to enrollment. Faster screening may increase throughput but could risk overlooking subtle exclusion criteria, potentially admitting ineligible participants and compromising data integrity.
options: Implement a structured digital intake questionnaire that automatically flags exclusion criteria before the clinical interview.
Deploy a dedicated screening nurse to conduct rapid bedside assessments during the first visit, ensuring immediate triage.
Introduce a peer-review panel of sleep specialists to evaluate screening decisions in real time, adding a second layer of oversight.
review: Tension between speed and accuracy; options miss the risk of over-reliance on automated flags that may miss nuanced clinical judgments.

name: Data Storage Scalability
consequences: Expanding storage capacity will accommodate growing sensor data volumes and enable longer retention periods. However, larger storage incurs higher maintenance costs and may introduce latency in data retrieval for analysis.
options: Transition to a tiered storage architecture, keeping recent data on fast SSD arrays and archiving older data on cost-effective HDDs.
Adopt a cloud-based backup service with regional redundancy to offload local storage demands.
Implement a data compression pipeline that reduces raw file sizes before ingestion, preserving fidelity while saving space.
review: Tension between local control and scalability; options overlook potential compliance issues with off-site cloud storage under German data protection laws.

name: Interdisciplinary Collaboration Structure
consequences: Formalizing collaboration across neurology, psychology, engineering, and data science will accelerate tool development and broaden expertise. Yet, increased coordination may slow decision-making and dilute accountability.
options: Establish a rotating steering committee that meets monthly, with representatives from each discipline to prioritize tasks.
Create cross-functional working groups focused on specific deliverables, such as event-triage or sensor integration.
Introduce a shared digital workspace where all team members can co-author documents and track progress in real time.
review: Tension between integration and efficiency; options miss the challenge of aligning differing disciplinary timelines and publication cultures.

name: Regulatory Compliance Cadence
consequences: Adjusting the frequency of ethics and regulatory reviews will affect project flexibility and risk exposure. More frequent reviews may catch issues early but increase administrative burden.
options: Schedule quarterly ethics board submissions with interim progress reports to maintain transparency.
Implement a continuous compliance monitoring system that flags potential violations in real time.
Outsource regulatory documentation to a specialized compliance firm to streamline submissions.
review: Tension between oversight and agility; options overlook the cost and potential loss of institutional control when outsourcing.

name: Participant Experience Design
consequences: Enhancing the residential environment to feel more home-like can improve sleep quality and data validity. However, increased comfort may reduce the perceived clinical rigor, affecting participant adherence to protocols.
options: Integrate adjustable lighting and soundscapes that mimic natural sleep cycles while maintaining safety standards.
Offer personalized room amenities, such as preferred bedding and temperature control, to increase participant satisfaction.
Conduct periodic participant feedback surveys to iteratively refine environmental features and protocol adherence.
review: Tension between naturalistic data and protocol fidelity; options miss the risk of participants altering behavior due to perceived leniency.

name: Scientific Output Strategy
consequences: Prioritizing publication and grant writing will secure future funding and disseminate findings. Yet, focusing on output may divert resources from data collection and quality control.
options: Assign a dedicated scientific writer to draft manuscripts and grant proposals, freeing researchers for data work.
Schedule quarterly internal review sessions where preliminary results are critiqued before external submission.
Leverage open-access preprint servers to share early findings, stimulating community feedback and potential collaborations.
review: Tension between rapid dissemination and rigorous peer review; options overlook the risk of premature data release compromising study integrity.
